text: Hello friends. This is a case of laparoscopic cholecystectomy for a patient suffering from chronic cholecystitis and have a very large stone. This is the MRI which is showing that approximately four point six centimeter stone is there.

Two elliptical incisions are made over the umbilicus and the inverted inferior crease using an #11 blade. The surgeon inserts a Veress needle into the incision, lifting the abdominal wall and positioning the needle perpendicular to it, aimed towards the anus. Two audible clicks are heard, followed by an irrigation test.

The surgeon performs the hanging drop test, followed by connecting the tubing of the insufflator to begin insufflation. The preset pressure is set at fifteen with a flow rate of one liter per minute. As the insufflation progresses, the actual pressure increases until it reaches the preset pressure, requiring between 1.5 to 6 liters of gas. Once the pneumoperitoneum is complete, the surgeon removes the Veress needle and enlarges the skin incision over the inferior crease of the umbilicus using a small mosquito forceps.

The surgeon dilates the vitreous into a channel track using the Scandinavian technique. The trocar is held like a pistol, with its head resting on a thin hard eminence. Using a screwing motion, the surgeon introduces the first primary trocar into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon introduces a telescope and observes extensive adhesions in the abdominal cavity. Due to the lack of space for an epigastric port, a trocar is placed in the left hypochondrium to facilitate adhesiolysis. Using a harmonic scalpel, the surgeon begins to separate adhesions, noting the left lobe of the liver attached to the stomach and costal margins. The falciform ligament and right lobe of the liver are identified, but the gallbladder remains obscured by adhesions.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis using a harmonic device to separate adhesions around the gallbladder and stomach. The adhesions, resulting from previous episodes of cholecystitis, are carefully dissected from the inferior margin of the liver and omentum. The harmonic device is positioned with its silicone tip facing the gallbladder to prevent puncture during the procedure.

The surgeon displays the gallbladder, which is impacted with a large stone measuring approximately 4.6 to 8 centimeters. Adhesions around the fundus region have been mostly separated. In the Calot's triangle, remaining adhesions are being carefully separated using the left hand to hold the omentum and the right hand to perform the dissection. The stomach is visible below the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a fundus clasp to hold the gallbladder fundus and retract it towards the right shoulder, facilitating the separation of adhesions between the duodenum and stomach.

The surgeon identifies a band between the stomach and the cystic pedicle, pushing it down to reveal the common bile duct (CBD) and duodenum below. Anteromedial traction is applied to the Hartmann's pouch to open the posterior peritoneum above the Rouviere's sulcus. The gallbladder is partially separated from the liver, with additional adhesions being dissected to facilitate posterior peritoneum dissection. The stomach is further mobilized, and the peritoneum between the gallbladder and liver is separated using anteromedial traction.

The surgeon applies anteromedial traction to separate the posterior peritoneum above the roviate sulcus, then shifts to anterolateral traction to separate the peritoneum and one-third of the gallbladder body. The dissection progresses downward, creating a posterior window. Streaming of the peritoneum around the cystic pedicle is performed to create an anterior window, allowing for the definition of Calot's triangle. The inferior surface of the liver is visible, forming one boundary of Calot's triangle.

The surgeon separates the peritoneum over the cystic pedicle, achieving skeletonization of the cystic duct and cystic artery. Below, the common bile duct (CBD) is visible, indicating the precise anatomical location of the dissection.

The surgeon forms the anterior window by separating the peritoneum over the cystic pedicle using a harmonic device. The Maryland instrument is then used to enlarge the anterior window by opening its jaws, creating a 2.2 cm space between the artery and duct. The posterior window is visible between the artery and the liver's inferior surface. An extracorporeal Misrach knot is fed into the window, tied outside with sequential hitches, winds, and locks.
The surgeon uses a knot pusher to tighten the extracorporeal knot near the common bile duct (CBD). The knot is then cut, and a surgical clip is applied to secure the area.

A titanium clip is applied to the cystic artery, ensuring both jaws of the clip are visible. The harmonic scalpel is used to cut the artery, with the vibrating jaw kept under vision and the Teflon jaw away. The cystic pedicle is separated, and the gallbladder is detached from the liver using anteromedial and anterolateral traction. The peritoneal shoulders are cut, and areolar tissue is separated, revealing a large stone within the gallbladder. An endobag is used for stone removal.

The surgeon places a commercially available endo bag inside the surgical field and opens its mouth. The gallbladder, containing a single large stone, is introduced into the endo bag. Using ovum forceps, the stone is crushed into smaller pieces to facilitate removal. The rectus is stretched with large jaw artery forceps, and the gallbladder stone is further crushed inside the endo bag.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers the gallbladder containing a large stone out through the epigastric port using right, left, up, and down movements. The port is then closed, and the knot is tied externally to secure the closure.
